items.question_text: When ATC responds "STAND BY" to an operational request, what does that response mean?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-18, "STAND BY" acknowledges the request but does not approve or deny it.

choices.text: The request has been acknowledged, but it is neither approved nor denied yet.
The request is approved and the pilot should proceed as requested.
The request is denied unless the pilot repeats it immediately.
The controller is transferring responsibility for the request to the next sector.

items.question_text: If known, what should be included in a UAS activity advisory?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-22, include position, distance, course, type of unmanned aircraft, and altitude when known.

choices.text: Position, distance, course, type of unmanned aircraft, and altitude.
Only the airport name and whether the report came from a pilot or controller.
Position and altitude only, because equipment type is not operationally useful.
Only the direction of flight and whether the activity appears intentional.

items.question_text: For time-based management when workload permits, which type of automation clearance should be preferred?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-3, closed loop clearances are preferred over open loop clearances when workload permits.

choices.text: Closed loop clearances in preference to open loop clearances.
Open loop clearances in preference to closed loop clearances.
Manual clearances only, because automation should be avoided for TBM.
Any type of clearance, because the order does not express a preference.

items.question_text: If you receive a report of possible human trafficking under Blue Lightning guidance, who must be notified?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-31, the supervisor or controller-in-charge must be notified of reports of possible human trafficking.

choices.text: The supervisor or controller-in-charge.
Only airport law enforcement, with no internal notification required.
The next sector first, because the report is traffic-related.
No one until the reporting aircraft lands and confirms the suspicion.

items.question_text: If a condition may adversely affect air safety, what should you do with that information?
items.explanation: Per 2-1-9, conditions that may adversely affect air safety should be reported as soon as possible to the appropriate office.

choices.text: Report it as soon as possible to the appropriate FSS, airport manager's office, ARTCC, or approach control facility.
Wait until the end of the shift so the report can be consolidated with routine notes.
Pass it only to the next controller working the position, since outside notifications are optional.
Report it only if a pilot specifically asks for the information on frequency.

items.question_text: If a flight plan is filed directly with the center, how must the information provided by the pilot be captured?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-1, all items provided by the pilot must be recorded on a strip, by data entry, or on a voice recorder.

choices.text: Record all items on a flight progress strip, by flight data entry, or on a voice recorder.
Write down only the route and requested altitude unless the pilot asks for a full record.
Accept the information verbally without recording it if the frequency is busy.
Record the call sign only, then retrieve the rest from the pilot after departure.

items.question_text: How must flight-plan data be forwarded when a proposed route includes more than 20 elements external to the originating ARTCC's area?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-10, NADIN must be used to forward the data to all affected centers.

choices.text: Use NADIN to forward the data to all affected centers.
Relay the route verbally to only the first affected center.
Use any local coordination method because NADIN is optional in this case.
Wait until the aircraft is airborne, then issue amendments sector by sector.

items.question_text: When may "NRP" be entered in the remarks section of a flight plan?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-15, "NRP" must not be entered unless prior coordination is accomplished with the ATCSCC or international NRP procedures prescribe it.

choices.text: Only when prior coordination has been accomplished with the ATCSCC or when prescribed by international NRP flight operations procedures.
Any time the controller expects reroutes or weather deviations later in the flight.
Whenever the aircraft was previously moved for tactical reasons, even without coordination.
Only on military overseas flights, because civilian flights may not use the code at all.

items.question_text: Where should proposed tower en route flight plans and related amendments be forwarded?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-2, they are forwarded to the appropriate departure terminal facility.

choices.text: To the appropriate departure terminal facility.
To the arrival terminal facility serving the destination airport.
Directly to the ARTCC that owns the final en route segment.
To the pilot's FSS only, because tower en route plans are not terminal data.

items.question_text: When should aircraft departure times be forwarded to FSSs or military operations offices?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-3, departure times are forwarded when those facilities have requested them.

choices.text: When those facilities have requested them.
After every departure, whether requested or not.
Only when the departure was delayed more than 30 minutes.
Only when the aircraft is changing from VFR to IFR after takeoff.

items.question_text: If a pilot wants to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan, what should you do?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-5, request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS.

choices.text: Request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS.
Cancel the IFR flight plan on the pilot's behalf without further coordination.
Direct the pilot to remain IFR until landing because ATC must approve the change.
Tell the pilot to call the departure controller back after leaving the airspace.

items.question_text: If a different altitude is still desired after an aircraft has crossed one facility's area, who reinitiates that altitude request?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-6, the pilot reinitiates the request with the next facility.

choices.text: The pilot reinitiates it with the next facility.
The previous controller must keep forwarding the request until it is granted.
The receiving controller must automatically issue the requested altitude.
No one reinitiates it because the original request remains active across all facilities.

items.question_text: Unless otherwise authorized in a facility directive, what should be used to post current data on air traffic and required clearances?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-1, flight progress strips are used unless a facility directive authorizes otherwise.

choices.text: Flight progress strips.
Unofficial scratch notes only.
Pilot readbacks alone, without any posted data.
Verbal coordination only, because posted data are optional.

items.question_text: How should aircraft type be entered on flight data?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-6, use the approved aircraft type designator in accordance with FAA Order 7360.1.

choices.text: Use the approved aircraft type designator in accordance with FAA Order 7360.1.
Use any common nickname for the aircraft as long as controllers understand it locally.
Spell out the manufacturer and model each time rather than using a designator.
Use the pilot's company call sign instead of the aircraft type.

items.question_text: How should an aircraft's clearance status be shown on flight progress data?
items.explanation: Per 2-3-9, use an appropriate clearance symbol followed by a dash and other pertinent information.

choices.text: Use an appropriate clearance symbol followed by a dash and other pertinent information.
Use only plain-language notes with no symbol or shorthand.
Use a dash alone, because the symbol is implied by the route.
Use the aircraft type designator in place of a clearance symbol.

items.question_text: When tower positions are combined, which frequency should not be used for airborne communications?
items.explanation: Per 2-4-1, do not use ground control frequency for airborne communications when positions are combined in the tower.

choices.text: Ground control frequency.
Local control frequency.
ATIS frequency.
The departure controller's frequency only after takeoff.

items.question_text: After an actual emergency has passed, what priority should messages relating to that accident receive?
items.explanation: Per 2-4-10, once the emergency has passed, those messages receive lower priority.

choices.text: Lower priority.
Top priority until the aircraft is parked at the gate.
Equal priority with ongoing emergency traffic.
No priority at all, because follow-up messages may not be transmitted.

items.question_text: Which words may be used to interrupt lower-priority interphone messages when necessary?
items.explanation: Per 2-4-11, use the words "emergency" or "control."

choices.text: "Emergency" or "control."
"Priority" or "attention."
"Break" only, because it always outranks other terms.
"Urgent" only, because emergency is reserved for aircraft.

items.question_text: What is the normal monitoring requirement for interphones and assigned radio frequencies?
items.explanation: Per 2-4-2, interphones and assigned radio frequencies are monitored continuously.

choices.text: Monitor them continuously.
Monitor them only when traffic volume is high.
Monitor interphones continuously, but radio frequencies only on pilot request.
Monitor only the published primary frequency unless a supervisor directs otherwise.

items.question_text: Which transmissions are authorized on ATC communications channels?
items.explanation: Per 2-4-5, transmit only messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.

choices.text: Only messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.
Any operationally interesting information, even if it does not affect ATC or safety.
Company messages whenever the frequency is not busy.
Routine administrative traffic if all controllers on frequency agree.

items.question_text: If deceptive or phantom transmissions are being received in an area, what must be broadcast to aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 2-4-6, broadcast an alert to aircraft operating on all frequencies within the affected area.

choices.text: An alert on all frequencies within the affected area.
A warning only on the primary local frequency, because cross-frequency alerts are optional.
A request for pilots to ignore all ATC instructions until the source is found.
A message to FSS only, because aircraft are not advised directly.

items.question_text: If a fix is not named, how should it be stated?
items.explanation: Per 2-5-3, state the name of the NAVAID followed by the specified radial, localizer, or azimuth.

choices.text: State the name of the NAVAID followed by the specified radial, localizer, or azimuth.
State only the mileage from the airport and let the pilot infer the bearing.
Use the nearest airway identifier instead of the NAVAID reference.
State the NAVAID name only, because the course element is optional.

items.question_text: On what basis must controllers disseminate weather data?
items.explanation: Per 2-6-6, dissemination is based on the operational impact on the sector or area of control jurisdiction.

choices.text: The operational impact on the sector or area of control jurisdiction.
The amount of detail available in the weather product, regardless of operational effect.
Whether the data originated inside the facility's own weather district only.
Whether the pilot specifically asks for every weather element on frequency.

items.question_text: Where must current altimeter settings be obtained from?
items.explanation: Per 2-7-1, obtain them from direct-reading instruments or directly from weather reporting stations.

choices.text: From direct-reading instruments or directly from weather reporting stations.
From any nearby aircraft that recently reported a local setting.
From the last ATIS broadcast even if a newer official setting is available.
From any center sector that worked traffic over the airport earlier in the day.

items.question_text: When RVR is issued along with other weather elements, how should it be transmitted?
items.explanation: Per 2-8-3, RVR values are transmitted in the normal sequence used for weather reporting.

choices.text: In the normal sequence used for weather reporting.
Before all other weather elements, because it always has priority.
After the altimeter setting only, regardless of the rest of the weather report.
Separately from the weather report so it is not mixed with other elements.

items.question_text: If a recorded broadcast has been interrupted for more than 12 hours, how is the first resumed message identified?
items.explanation: Per 2-9-1, identify the first resumed message with "Alpha" or the first assigned alphabet letter word.

choices.text: With "Alpha" or the first assigned alphabet letter word.
With the last letter used before the interruption, so the sequence stays continuous.
With "Zulu" to show the broadcast restarted after a long outage.
With no identifier change unless the broadcast was interrupted for more than 24 hours.

items.question_text: When should an aircraft be informed of an observed abnormal condition?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-10, provide the information when requested by the pilot or when you deem it necessary.

choices.text: When the pilot requests it or when you deem it necessary.
Only after another aircraft confirms the same abnormality.
Only after the aircraft has landed and cleared the runway.
Only if maintenance personnel first verify the abnormal condition.

items.question_text: While providing preventive control service, when should advice or instructions be issued?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-2, issue advice or instructions only if a situation develops that requires corrective action.

choices.text: Only if a situation develops that requires corrective action.
Continuously, so the aircraft never has to self-separate within the arrangement.
Only after the pilot requests ATC intervention.
Whenever the controller would prefer a different pattern flow, even without a conflict.

items.question_text: How should vehicles, equipment, or personnel on or near the movement area be described to pilots?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-6, the description should help the pilot recognize the traffic.

choices.text: In a manner that assists pilots in recognizing them.
Only by exact airport-grid coordinates, even if that is harder to interpret quickly.
Only by clock position, regardless of whether a surface reference would be clearer.
Only if the pilot specifically asks for movement-area traffic details.

items.question_text: If ATC cannot determine a position visually or by using a display system, what may be used instead?
items.explanation: Per 3-1-7, position reports may be used when visual or display-based determination is not possible.

choices.text: Position reports.
Assumptions based on the last known taxi route only.
The pilot's estimated time to the next intersection only.
A departure sequence estimate from ground control.

items.question_text: When do the sea-lane control rules apply?
items.explanation: Per 3-12-1, they apply wherever sea lanes are established and controlled.

choices.text: Wherever sea lanes are established and controlled.
Only at military bases that publish local seaplane letters of agreement.
Only when a seaplane is arriving IFR.
Only during daylight operations because sea lanes are a visual procedure.

items.question_text: When are ATC light signals used to control aircraft, vehicles, equipment, or personnel on the movement area?
items.explanation: Per 3-2-1, ATC light signals are used when radio communications cannot be employed.

choices.text: When radio communications cannot be employed.
Whenever a light signal would be faster than using the radio.
Only at night, because daytime light signals are not authorized.
Only for vehicles and personnel, not for aircraft.

items.question_text: If a tailwind component exists when authorizing runway use, what must be stated?
items.explanation: Per 3-5-3, always state both wind direction and velocity when a tailwind component exists.

choices.text: Both wind direction and velocity.
Wind direction only, because the pilot can judge the strength visually.
Velocity only, because the tailwind component is the controlling factor.
Neither one unless the pilot asks whether a tailwind exists.

items.question_text: When the system generates an ASDE safety alert, what is the controller's immediate responsibility?
items.explanation: Per 3-6-4, the controller must immediately assess the situation visually and as presented on the system.

choices.text: Immediately assess the situation visually and as presented by the system.
Wait for the next update cycle to confirm the alert before acting.
Ignore the alert unless a pilot also reports a runway conflict.
Transfer responsibility to ground control before evaluating the alert.

items.question_text: How is an aircraft cleared for touch-and-go, stop-and-go, or low approach treated until it touches the runway?
items.explanation: Per 3-8-2, it is considered an arriving aircraft until touchdown.

choices.text: As an arriving aircraft until it touches the runway.
As a departing aircraft as soon as the clearance is issued.
As neither arriving nor departing until the pilot states the intended maneuver again.
As a departing aircraft only if the pilot intends to remain in the pattern.
